does: Flash-DMD presents a framework for accelerating diffusion models through efficient timestep distillation combined with joint reinforcement learning refinement. The core contribution is a timestep-aware distillation strategy that reduces training cost to 2.1% of prior methods like DMD2 while maintaining image quality, paired with a joint training scheme where RL-based fine-tuning (for objectives like aesthetic appeal) occurs simultaneously with ongoing distillation. The method addresses the instability and reward hacking problems typical in RL-based model refinement by leveraging the stable, well-defined distillation loss as a regularizing anchor during optimization. Results demonstrate both faster convergence and improved fidelity compared to existing acceleration approaches.
connects: This work extends the line of research on diffusion model acceleration through knowledge distillation, building directly on timestep distillation methods like DMD and DMD2 that compress multi-step generation into fewer sampling steps. It combines this with recent efforts to apply reinforcement learning to fine-tune generative models for human preferences and specific quality metrics—a challenging problem where naive RL approaches suffer from instability and mode collapse. The paper responds to the known tension between these two objectives: distillation requires extensive training, while RL fine-tuning on distilled models is notoriously unstable. Flash-DMD synthesizes insights from both domains, treating distillation not merely as a preprocessing step but as an ongoing regularization mechanism during RL training.
enables: This work enables practical deployment of high-quality diffusion models in computationally constrained settings where few-step generation is critical. By stabilizing RL-based refinement through joint distillation training, it opens pathways for efficiently adapting models to user preferences, domain-specific aesthetic criteria, or safety objectives without sacrificing generation quality. The demonstrated approach could extend to other generative models beyond diffusion and to multi-objective optimization scenarios where balancing competing goals (speed, quality, user preference) is necessary. Future work might explore scaling this joint training framework to larger models, extending it to other reward signals beyond aesthetics, and investigating theoretical guarantees on convergence and reward hacking prevention.
